items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: What determines whether a star becomes a white dwarf or a neutron star?
items.correct: Its mass - low/medium mass becomes a white dwarf; massive stars produce neutron stars or black holes.
items.misconception: Its colour during its main sequence phase.
items.why: Students think colour determines end state. Mass is the key factor - low-mass stars (like the Sun) end as white dwarfs; high-mass stars supernova, leaving neutron stars or black holes depending on mass.

items.q: Why are Type Ia supernovae used as 'standard candles'?
items.correct: They have nearly uniform peak brightness, so their apparent brightness reveals distance.
items.misconception: Because they happen at regular time intervals.
items.random_wrong: Because they are the brightest objects in the universe.
items.why: Students confuse standard candle with regular timing. The key property is CONSISTENT INTRINSIC BRIGHTNESS - if you know the actual luminosity, the observed dimness tells you the distance.

items.q: What is the principle behind stellar parallax?
items.correct: A nearby star appears to shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: Stars actually move position throughout the year.
items.why: Students think stars literally move. Parallax is an APPARENT shift due to our changing viewpoint - same reason your finger seems to jump when you alternate eyes. The closer the star, the bigger the shift.

items.q: What does Hubble's Law state?
items.correct: More distant galaxies recede faster - their recession velocity is proportional to distance.
items.misconception: All galaxies move at the same speed.
items.random_wrong: Galaxies only move in random directions.
items.why: Students miss the proportionality. The expansion isn't motion through space - space itself stretches, so widely separated galaxies appear to move apart faster than nearer ones.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: What is the cosmic microwave background?
items.correct: Relic radiation from when the universe became transparent, about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
items.misconception: Background noise from human microwave ovens.
items.random_wrong: Light from the most distant galaxies.
items.why: Students take 'microwave' as the appliance. The CMB is photons from the early universe, now stretched to microwave wavelengths by expansion - the earliest light we can detect, predicted by Big Bang theory.

items.q: Why do astronomers infer the existence of dark matter?
items.correct: Visible matter alone cannot account for galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing.
items.misconception: Dark matter has been directly observed in telescopes.
items.random_wrong: Because the universe looks dark at night.
items.why: Students think dark matter has been seen. It's INFERRED from gravitational effects on visible matter and light - galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone, implying unseen matter contributes most gravity.

items.q: What does dark energy explain?
items.correct: The observed acceleration of the universe's expansion.
items.misconception: Why some galaxies are darker than others.
items.why: Students confuse dark energy with darkness in general. Dark energy is a hypothetical component driving the universe's expansion to SPEED UP over time - a discovery from supernova observations in the 1990s.

items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: What causes Earth's seasons?
items.correct: Earth's axial tilt - 23.5 degrees - causes different hemispheres to receive more direct sunlight at different times of year.
items.misconception: Earth is closer to the Sun in summer.
items.random_wrong: The Sun's intensity changes throughout the year.
items.why: Students assume distance causes seasons, but Earth is closest to the Sun in January (Northern Hemisphere winter). The TILT changes the angle of sunlight and day length - that's what drives seasons.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.misconception: Eclipses happen randomly.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: What is the difference between a solar eclipse and a lunar eclipse?
items.correct: In a solar eclipse the Moon blocks the Sun; in a lunar eclipse Earth's shadow covers the Moon.
items.misconception: Solar eclipses happen by day, lunar at night - that is the only difference.
items.why: Students give a superficial answer. The mechanism differs: solar = Moon between Sun and Earth (we're in Moon's shadow); lunar = Earth between Sun and Moon (Moon enters Earth's shadow).

items.q: What is a light year?
items.correct: A unit of DISTANCE - the distance light travels in one year.
items.misconception: A unit of time.
items.why: Students hear 'year' and assume time. Light year is distance (about 9.46 trillion km) - it's a useful astronomical scale, not a measure of when something happened.

items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.correct: Light-gathering ability and angular resolution.
items.misconception: Magnification.
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.

items.q: How do astronomers determine the chemical composition of a distant star?
items.correct: By analysing absorption lines in its spectrum.
items.misconception: By sending probes to collect samples.
items.random_wrong: By measuring its colour with the naked eye.
items.why: Students think we can only know about local things. Each element has unique spectral fingerprints. Star light passes through outer layers; specific wavelengths are absorbed, revealing what elements are present.

items.q: What is a black hole's event horizon?
items.correct: The boundary beyond which not even light can escape.
items.misconception: The visible 'edge' of the black hole.
items.random_wrong: A literal horizon you can walk to.
items.why: Students picture a surface. The event horizon is a mathematical BOUNDARY - nothing is physically there. Once inside, escape requires faster-than-light speed, which is impossible.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.q: What shape are planetary orbits?
items.correct: Ellipses (Kepler's first law), with the Sun at one focus.
items.misconception: Perfect circles.
items.why: Students think orbits are circular. Most are nearly circular, but Kepler showed they're ELLIPTICAL - this small difference explains why Earth-Sun distance varies through the year.

items.q: What is the 'habitable zone' around a star?
items.correct: The region where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.
items.misconception: Where life definitely exists.
items.random_wrong: Where humans could survive without spacesuits.
items.why: Students assume habitable zone = inhabited or human-suitable. It's a NECESSARY but not sufficient condition for Earth-like life - based purely on temperature for liquid water; doesn't guarantee atmosphere or life.

items.q: What is the difference between an asteroid and a comet?
items.correct: Asteroids are rocky/metallic; comets contain ice that vaporises near the Sun, producing a tail.
items.misconception: They are the same object with different names.
items.why: Students treat both as 'space rocks'. The composition differs: comets have volatile ice that sublimes when warmed near the Sun (creating coma and tail); asteroids are rocky/metallic with no tail.

items.q: What is the current best estimate for the age of the universe?
items.correct: About 13.8 billion years.
items.misconception: About 6,000 years.
items.why: Students may hold non-scientific views or guess. The current consensus from CMB measurements and stellar ages is approximately 13.8 billion years - the time since the Big Bang.

items.q: Why was Pluto reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006?
items.correct: It does not clear its orbital neighbourhood of similar-sized bodies.
items.misconception: It is too small to be a planet.
items.why: Students remember size as the reason. The IAU defined three criteria for planets - Pluto fails the third (orbital dominance), as it shares its region with other Kuiper Belt objects of similar size.
